author: @jvon3885
text: The truth about those hieroglyphs is that they were all about electrical harvesting. The pyramids were power generators and I think they said they were tombs was to throw us off. They literally don't want us to know how "free" energy (basically not having to pay a corporation free) was achieved before the great world disaster and downfall of certain empires. Those pics literally match a ton of symbols used for electrical engineering and such.

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: Reminds me of years ago when I was a kid we discovered you could feel the AC induction through your skin. I had a water bed which had an ungrounded heater mat. If you wore certain soled shoes and run the back of your finger along my stomach you could feel a buzzing sensation. I repeated this a few years ago with my mothers electric blanket. This time I had a multimeter, If she held one probe and I held the meter and the other probe in the air we would see a voltage oscillating at 50Hz. What was strange was when I changed it to measure the current it showed a draw of 2A. Again, it changed if I had shoes on or not and if I was holding the meter or not. So strange

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Jehu McSpooran it's an odd world - basically we are living inside a huge capacitor formed between the ground and the ionosphere - see what tesla had to say about this stuff - you'll find it fascinating

replies.author: @JehuMcSpooran
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Aye, odd it is. Basically I came to the conclusion that when lying on an electric blanket, your body acts as a great big semi liquid secondary of a transformer with the blanket acting as the primary. I suppose then the multimeter leads are acting as a broadcast antenna.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Jehu McSpooran i guess so - but i think it is more likely that your acting as a cap mate

author: @bridgendesar
text:
Wasn't that the way TV detector vans were supposed to work?
Am I right in thinking there is also a problem with rectifying very low voltages aka the forward voltage drop across a diode?
And where's my techno music?

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: bridgendesar haha, no techno music yet, you'll have to keep your ears cupped for that one. The forward voltage of standard "jellybean" diodes drop 0.7-0.5V so you lose that, but you can get tons of diodes with low forward voltage. This is why you hear people going on about germanium diodes since they have a forward voltage drop of approx 0.15V but are quite sensitive. I blew a few up playing around with them. Personally I'd just use shottky diodes as they have a voltage drop of as lows 0.15 but they are robust and available in a wide range of powers.

replies.author: @bridgendesar
replies.text: Are Roberts graphene diodes any good?

author: @EVZebra
text:
Hi Robert, it is useful and interesting, I have a design challenge for you to overcome.
Using basic readily available materials, could you please have a go at making some thing that can collect energy from the sun, that goes straight into a battery.
I know that the rich and famous can just buy solar panels, but for 3rd world applications can you think of a way to do it?
Even if its not that efficient it doesnt matter as the energy from the sun is free and there is lots of space in third world countries so we could just make it bigger to make up for the lack of efficiency. This would be a great project as it has real world applications which will help a lot of people who dont even have electricity in their homes. Can it be done? Thanks.

author: @TheBaconWizard
text:
Several experiments I tried while living in a yurt for anyone that is looking at alternative energy sources :

Earth Battery: rubbish beyond words and the better versions were hard work, need watering and were still rubbish. Seriously, forget it.

Tree Battery: worked-ish but would eventually kill the tree (all to light an LED! Wooo!) and not reliable either. 

Wind energy: Sure in the right place but needed a better battery to store it. Bulky too. Worked on a fold-away canvass one. MegaCap maybe!

PV: As for wind


Solar Heating (of water) Really VERY good at the right time of year. Sadly, it was winter when I actually wanted it. 

Methane powered genny: Very good. Hard work though shovelling cow-crap. First catch your cow. Also, realised how inefficient my genny was at recharging batteries and what a waste it would be to just use the genny for one thing without also charging batteries. 

Thermovoltaic: Bloody superb since I had to have the fire-on anyway. Saved my damn life.

Really surprising (but we aren't running anything big, just trickle-charging a NiCad over a week) was collecting RF under power lines. Easy peasy.

Joule-thief was very handy, everyone should know what it is and how to get or make several. 

Never got round to a gasifier that might use waste of some kind, to my regret. Too complicated a project to fettle at the time and was scared of constantly knackering my emergency generator with dirty gas :)

The overall lesson I learned was that we badly need much better and affordable (and sustainably made) batteries or super-caps, and then it all becomes entirely viable to be an eco-warrior and still live a fairly normal life. 

Thanks for all the work Rob and team are doing. It's very exciting.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: TheBaconWizard cool, lots of useful info their mate, thanks for sharing

author: @Slider2732
text:
Try this one fellas - a few cm of copper wire soldered to the join of 2 opposite facing 1N4148 diodes (commonly called an AV plug). Attach a superbright white LED to the free ends of the diodes. Hold the setup near to a CFL bulb and the LED will light.
Remove the LED and you'll easily see up to 25V on the multimeter.
By holding a diode, you become a virtual ground. Can replace your physical connection with a large heatsink, or an actual ground stake in the Earth.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: slider2732 interesting idea, cheers mate

text: nice examples, i have found it to be usefull to check more ways to be sure of what efects you get. But as a response the the experiment on hanging a receaver higher increses energy collected is actually true if you use a propper static charce collector. author: @cooltroops8182
text: What if you use a yagi antenna array ,think you will get more energy because of its reflector ?!

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: cool troops almost certainly the yagi is a good design for TV frequencies and the reflector helps. it's worth nothing that antenna design is still a bit of a black art. if you make an "antenna" for light, it's easy because light travels pretty much in a straight line, but the lower the frequency, the more you bend around corners and the more you permeate solid objects so it becomes much more difficult to visualise what's going on. very interesting however, fascinating in fact

author: @MichelPASTOR
text: Good idea. Looks ideal for a low consumption device like a smoke detector. Look at LTC3108 IC Ultralow Voltage Step-Up Converter and Power Manager, it's designed to do energy harvesting, starts up at 20mV and can output several mA.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: Michel PASTOR cheers mate, incidentally I've got a couple of those chips on my desk. I've already deadbugged one and have been playing with it. The only caveat is the 1:100 step up transformer you need for the 25mv input since these can be expensive. Interestingly the same can be achieved with a jfet based joulethief and a step up transformer. i think the key here is very low voltage oscillation to drive the boost converter. there are a bunch of known oscillators out there which I'm taking a look at

replies.author: @benwinkel
replies.text: +Ashley Mills Interesting! Would like to know more about that jfet-oscillator. I've got a couple of oscillators running off the stray energy coming from the back of my tv. One is a blocking oscillator and one is a joule thief. Simply connect a coil of wire through an av-plug to a cap and start harvesting! :)
